discovery.text: From Allan B. Jacobs' Great Streets (1993). The definitive example of a European cours — the public outdoor room. Jacobs returns to it as a model for proportional perfection.

narrative.summary: The definitive example of a European cours — the public outdoor room. Jacobs returns to it as a model for proportional perfection.

blocks.title: Why Jacobs Chose It
blocks.body: 140-foot wide boulevard with four rows of plane trees creating a cathedral-like canopy, fountains at intervals, cafés on the south side (sunny), hôtels particuliers on the north (shaded). Les Deux Garçons, where Cézanne drank coffee, is still here.

blocks.title: How to Walk It
blocks.body: Walk the full length. Jacobs is explicit: you have to walk a great street to understand it — the sequence, the proportions, how the edges change. Give it an hour minimum. Sit at a café or on a bench midway. Notice what makes it feel alive.

blocks.name: D'Amour
blocks.why: Terrace directly on the Cours — the cocktail bar that pulls the street's social life into the evening. The Cours Mirabeau's promenade quality depends on these terraces spilling onto the pavement.

blocks.name: Restaurant Côté Cour
blocks.why: Mediterranean and French cuisine steps from the Fontaine de la Rotonde. The lower end of the Cours, where the street opens to the great fountain — Jacobs' terminal element that gives the promenade its sense of arrival.

blocks.name: Café Caumont
blocks.why: The café of the Hôtel de Caumont arts centre, one block off the Cours. One of Provence's finest 18th-century hôtels particuliers, now an exhibition venue. The institutional anchor that draws visitors off the boulevard into the hinterland of great streets.
